claimed_contribution: The paper provides a conceptual definition of product incongruity based on associative links and distinguishes moderate from extreme incongruity by whether resolution can rely on preexisting shared associations.

It identifies cognitive flexibility as a moderator that can increase acceptance of extremely incongruent new products, demonstrated with three manipulations: positive affect, future (vs past) framing, and a cognitive-flexibility prime.

It shows that incongruity resolution mediates evaluation effects and presents evidence that understanding the product benefit (insight), rather than affect from the resolution process, drives higher evaluations of extremely incongruent products.
context: The studies examine consumer reactions to hypothetical new product concepts in familiar categories, especially beverages and household products (e.g., vitamin-fortified vodka, black toilet paper, carbonated milk). The focal setting is innovations that keep a known category frame but introduce an attribute that conflicts strongly with category expectations.
design_and_data: The paper reports four between-subjects experiments with undergraduate participants using product-concept descriptions. Study 1 (N=299) manipulates affect (positive vs neutral) and incongruity level (congruent, moderate, extreme; two product replicates) and measures incongruity resolution with coded open-ended responses and a two-item scale. Study 2 (N=125) and Study 3 (N=149) test positive affect and temporal framing (future vs past) effects on evaluations and incongruity resolution for moderate vs extreme products, including mediated-moderation and bootstrap indirect-effect tests. Study 4 (N=134) manipulates cognitive-flexibility priming (multiple vs single explanations) and benefit-rationale ad copy for an extremely incongruent product, and measures evaluation, resolution, perceived incongruity, and affect.
main_findings: Positive (vs neutral) affect increased participants' ability to make sense of extremely incongruent products and increased their evaluations of those products, while having no comparable effect for moderately incongruent products.
Describing a launch in the future (vs past) increased evaluations of an extremely incongruent product, and incongruity resolution mediated this interaction.

In the prime-plus-copy study, cognitively flexible participants rated the extremely incongruent product favorably with or without a provided rationale, whereas cognitively inflexible participants improved only when a rationale was provided; measured affect did not differ across these conditions.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper shows that increasing cognitive flexibility helps consumers resolve extreme attribute-category incongruity and thereby improves evaluations of highly novel products.
relation_to_prior_work: extends schema incongruity research by moving beyond the usual finding that extreme incongruity is evaluated negatively to identify conditions under which it can be evaluated positively. It complements prior work on really new products by focusing on attribute-based incongruity (the "what of it" problem) rather than only category identification. It also challenges an affect-only interpretation of resolution by presenting evidence consistent with an insight-based mechanism.
research_gap: Prior work largely documents that moderately incongruent products are preferred over extremely incongruent ones, but offers limited guidance on how to increase acceptance of extremely incongruent products. The paper also notes limited attention to the "what of it" problem - understanding the benefit of an incongruent attribute - and limited a priori differentiation between moderate and extreme incongruity.
research_question: How can firms increase consumer acceptance of new products whose differentiating attributes are extremely incongruent with category expectations? The paper tests whether cognitive flexibility helps consumers resolve extreme incongruity and whether this resolution improves evaluations. It also examines whether higher evaluations come from insight into benefits rather than affect generated by the resolution process.
